topic: A homeowner learns their house was a former crime scene
raw_output:
[story 1]

The dust motes danced in the slivers of afternoon light slicing through the grime-streaked windows. Mark dragged a damp cloth across the scarred oak floorboards, the sound of the rough movement grating on his nerves. He’d spent three weeks trying to get this place clean, to erase the lingering shadows that seemed to cling to every corner. Now, after the final layer of grime came off, a cold knot tightened in his stomach. He ran a hand over the peeling wallpaper in the hallway, his knuckles white. It wasn't just old; it felt *wrong*. He remembered the realtor’s casual dismissal, the quick sign-off, and now this oppressive silence, punctuated only by the creak of the settling foundation. He stared at a faint, almost invisible smear near the baseboard, a dark stain that refused to lift. "This isn't just old," he muttered to the empty room, his voice tight. "This is... history."

[story 2]

Sarah paced the perimeter of the living room, her heels clicking sharply against the hardwood. Every time she stopped, she’d press her palms against her temples, taking deep, ragged breaths. The air in the house felt thick, heavy, like breathing underwater. She’d hired the cleaners, the investigators, the whole arsenal of professionals, and nothing had changed. The lingering scent of stale mildew and something metallic, something sharp and unsettling, persisted no matter how many sprays she used. She knelt by the antique writing desk, her gaze fixed on a patch of discoloration on the wood surface. Her jaw clenched so hard she could feel the tension vibrating in her neck. "Come on," she whispered, her voice strained. "Just tell me what this is. Why can't I scrub it away?"

[story 3]

From the attic, David peered down the narrow stairwell, his shoulders slumped. He had spent the better part of the morning sifting through boxes of forgotten furniture, a pointless exercise in futility. He found a loose floorboard beneath the master bedroom, and when he pried it up, the smell hit him—a distinct, acrid tang that made his eyes water instantly. He pulled the board back out, revealing not insulation, but a faint, dried residue embedded in the wood grain. David dropped the board with a thud that echoed loudly in the quiet house. He leaned against the wall, breathing shallowly, his hands trembling as he processed the implication. He had bought a home; he hadn't bought a narrative.
